sources.notes: Statement and constraints: sum of first k digits of the infinite string 1234567891011..., with k up to 1e15.
Official solution confirms locating the containing number and summing digit contributions up to it.
Uses standard per-position digit counting for [1..n] plus the partial final number.
Independent derivation of locating the digit block and summing digit sums by formula/recursion.

annotation.summary: For the infinite decimal string formed by concatenating positive integers, answer the sum of the first k digits.
annotation.constraints: t <= 20000, k <= 1e15; decimal length is at most about 15, so per-digit formulas are enough.
annotation.core_idea: Subtract whole blocks of 1-digit, 2-digit, and longer numbers to find the exact number containing the k-th digit and the prefix length used inside it. Then compute the sum of digit sums of all complete numbers before it using positional digit-counting formulas, and add the needed prefix digits of the final number.
tricks: Block by number length in the Champernowne sequence.
Count digit contributions position by position.
Handle the partial final number separately.

solution_variants.name: digit-counting-formula
solution_variants.summary: Find the final number by decimal length blocks, compute sum_digits(1..num-1) with per-position digit counting, then add the prefix of num.

solution_variants.name: recursive-prefix-sum
solution_variants.summary: Equivalent recursive decomposition of digit-sum over [0..x] by leading digit and suffix interval.

tags.evidence: The main computation is a digit-position count for all numbers up to a boundary, equivalent to a compact digit-DP/counting formula.
The solution groups the infinite string by decimal length and reasons about decimal positional digits.
tags.description: Use positional base representation of an integer to derive counts, carries, digit sums, or canonical decompositions; applies when the base digits are central to the solution rather than just input parsing.
tags.created_reason: Existing math/number-theory is too broad for problems whose core is positional base decomposition and digit contribution counting.

tags.evidence: Digit sums over complete blocks are counted by combinatorial frequency formulas for each position.
